items.foco: Definition and pathophysiology of patent ductus arteriosus
items.title: Definition and pathophysiology
items.contentHtml:
Definition and pathophysiology

Patent ductus arteriosus is the lack of functional closure of the ductus arteriosus after birth. Pathophysiologically, in the fetus the duct diverts blood from the pulmonary artery to the aorta; At birth, the increase in arterial oxygen pressure and the drop in prostaglandins induce its closure in 48-96 hours. If it persists, a left-right shunt is generated from the aorta to the pulmonary artery, increasing pulmonary flow and left ventricular preload. The magnitude of the shunt depends on the size of the conduit and the relationship between pulmonary and systemic vascular resistance.

items.foco: Risk factors: prematurity and congenital rubella
items.title: Risk factors
items.contentHtml:
Risk factors

  • Prematurity: main factor; in newborns <30 weeks the incidence exceeds 30%. The immaturity of the ductal smooth muscle and the persistence of circulating prostaglandins prevent closure.
  • Congenital rubella: classic cause of persistence of the ductus arteriosus, associated with tetrad of malformations (heart disease, cataracts, deafness, microcephaly). The virus damages the ductal endothelium during the first trimester.
  • Other factors: neonatal hypoxia, respiratory distress syndrome, female sex (2:1) and history of patent ductus arteriosus in siblings.

items.title: Hemodynamic classification
items.contentHtml:
Hemodynamic classification

Hemodynamically, patent ductus arteriosus is classified according to the size of the left-right shunt and the impact on pulmonary circulation. Small: minimal shunt, without ventricular overload or pulmonary hypertension; The patient is usually asymptomatic. Moderate: significant shunt that causes left ventricular volume overload and increased pulmonary flow, with possible tachypnea and weight loss. Large: massive shunt leading to irreversible pulmonary hypertension (Eisenmenger syndrome) if not closed before age 2 years.

items.foco: Clinical manifestations: continuous murmur in machinery
items.title: Clinical manifestations
items.contentHtml:
Clinical manifestations

The classic finding is continuous machinery murmur (Gibson), audible at the high left sternal border, which is accentuated in systole and extends to diastole, radiating to the back. In premature infants, the murmur may only be systolic if pulmonary resistance is elevated. Bumping pulses and wide differential pressure (>50 mmHg) are signs of significant shunt. In small ducts, the murmur may be the only finding. Differential cyanosis (only in lower extremities) is rare and appears if there is severe pulmonary hypertension with reversal of the shunt.

items.foco: Signs of pulmonary hyperflow in infants
items.title: Signs of pulmonary hyperflow
items.contentHtml:
Signs of pulmonary hyperflow in infants

In infants with hemodynamically significant patent ductus arteriosus, pulmonary hyperflow manifests as tachypnea, intercostal retractions, nasal flaring and difficulty feeding with sweating. On auscultation: tachycardia, third sound and wet crackles at the lung bases. Radiologically: cardiomegaly with cardiothoracic index >0.55, increased pulmonary vascular network and prominent pulmonary artery sign. Color Doppler echocardiography confirms the shunt and estimates pulmonary pressure.

items.foco: Differential diagnosis with other congenital heart diseases
items.title: Differential diagnosis
items.contentHtml:
Differential diagnosis with other congenital heart diseases

  • Coronary arteriovenous fistula: continuous murmur but of atypical location (low precordium) and without jumping pulses.
  • Aortopulmonary window: similar continuous murmur, but the defect is in the proximal pulmonary artery; echocardiogram differentiates the location.
  • Ventricular communication with aortic insufficiency: back-and-forth murmur (systolic and diastolic, but not continuous) and signs of double lesion.
  • Silent patent ductus arteriosus: without audible murmur, detected only by echocardiogram; No intervention required.

items.foco: Color Doppler echocardiogram as gold standard
items.title: Gold standard diagnosis
items.contentHtml:
Color Doppler echocardiogram as gold standard

The color Doppler echocardiogram is the gold standard test to confirm the persistence of the ductus arteriosus. Visualize the conduit in the suprasternal or high parasternal plane, and color Doppler shows continuous turbulent flow from the descending aorta to the pulmonary artery. Measures the internal diameter of the duct (<1.5 mm small, 1.5-3 mm moderate, >3 mm large) and the pulmonary flow/systemic flow ratio (Qp/Qs). Additionally, it evaluates pulmonary artery pressure using the ductal flow velocity gradient. Transthoracic echocardiography is sufficient in most cases.

items.foco: Indications for pharmacological closure with indomethacin or ibuprofen
items.title: Pharmacological closure
items.contentHtml:
Indications for pharmacological closure with indomethacin or ibuprofen

Pharmacological closure is indicated in premature newborns (<32 weeks) with hemodynamically significant patent ductus arteriosus (left-right shunt, ventilator dependence or signs of pulmonary hyperflow). Drugs: intravenous indomethacin (0.1-0.2 mg/kg every 12-24 hours, 3 doses) or intravenous ibuprofen (initial dose 10 mg/kg, then 5 mg/kg at 24 and 48 hours). Contraindications: necrotizing enterocolitis, renal failure (creatinine >1.5 mg/dl), thrombocytopenia (<50,000/μl) and active intraventricular hemorrhage. Ibuprofen has fewer kidney adverse effects.

items.foco: Surgical closure vs. percutaneous with device
items.title: Surgical closure vs. percutaneous
items.contentHtml:
Surgical closure vs. percutaneous with device

In children over 6 months and adults, percutaneous closure with occlusive device (Amplatzer, coils) is the first line, with success >95% and lower morbidity. Indicated in ducts <8 mm in diameter. Surgical closure (ligation or videothoracoscopic section) is reserved for very large canals (>8 mm), unfavorable anatomy (window canal), contraindication for antiaggregation, or when percutaneous closure fails. In extremely premature babies (<1500 g), surgery is an option if pharmacological treatment fails. Complications of the device: embolization, hemolysis, obstruction of pulmonary branches.

items.foco: Complications: heart failure and pulmonary vascular disease
items.title: Complications
items.contentHtml:
Complications: heart failure and pulmonary vascular disease

Heart failure is the most common complication in infants with large shunt, manifested by tachypnea, hepatomegaly, edema and growth retardation. Requires treatment with diuretics (furosemide 1-2 mg/kg/dose) and sometimes digoxin. Pulmonary vascular disease (irreversible pulmonary hypertension) occurs if the left-right shunt does not close before the age of 2 years, leading to Eisenmenger syndrome (differential cyanosis, digital clubbing). Other complications: infective endocarditis, ductal aneurysm and ductal calcification.

items.title: Endocarditis and prophylaxis
items.contentHtml:
Infective endocarditis and antibiotic prophylaxis

The risk of infectious endocarditis in persistent uncomplicated ductus arteriosus is low (<0.5% annually), but increases if there is an audible murmur or pulmonary hypertension. Antibiotic prophylaxis is indicated only in patients with unrepaired patent ductus arteriosus who present previous endocarditis, valvular prosthesis or unrepaired cyanotic congenital heart disease. Routine prophylaxis is not recommended for dental procedures in repaired isolated canal without residual device. After percutaneous or surgical closure without residual prosthetic material, prophylaxis is not required after 6 months.

items.foco: Prognosis and follow-up according to age and size of the defect
items.title: Forecast and monitoring
items.contentHtml:
Prognosis and follow-up according to age and size of the defect

  • Small duct (<1.5 mm): excellent prognosis; Spontaneous closure possible up to one year of life. Annual clinical and echocardiographic follow-up until closure.
  • Moderate-large duct (>1.5 mm) in infants: requires closure (pharmacological, percutaneous or surgical) before 6-12 months to avoid pulmonary hypertension. After successful closure, excellent prognosis with normal life.
  • Unclosed duct in adults: risk of heart failure, endocarditis and pulmonary vascular disease; Percutaneous closure is recommended if feasible. Annual cardiological follow-up if not repaired.
